Why prince
Tokugawa main-line exception — its own clause, distinct from the sanke and Restoration-leader clauses



After the bakufu’s defeat in 1868 the Tokugawa main line was relocated from Edo to a 70-万-石 territory centred on Sunpu (modern Shizuoka). The defeated party was nevertheless granted prince rank in 1884 because the Kazoku-rei contained an explicit Tokugawa-main-line clause — separate from both the sanke clause (covering Owari/Kii/Mito) and the Restoration-leader clause (covering Shimazu/Mōri). 徳川家達 (1863–1940), great-grandson of the 11th shogun Ienari and grandson by adoption to the 14th shogun Iemochi, was the recipient.
The methodological point: the 1884 rules ennobled by what the rules said, not by who won the war. The defeated central party received the highest rank — because the rules said so.



18.9.2 B. Count → Marquess (3 cases)
Three families received marquess rather than the genmai-predicted count. One was the explicit sanke clause (Mito-Tokugawa); the other two were Restoration-loyalty rewards arriving years after the initial 1884 ennoblements (1888 for Echizen-Matsudaira/Fukui, 1891 for Date-Uwajima).

Why marquess
Sanke clause — explicit rule provision for the three Tokugawa cadet houses



Mito’s genmai of 58,553 koku sits squarely in count range. The 1884 Kazoku-rei nevertheless elevated the family to marquess because of an explicit “徳川旧三家” clause: the heads of the three sanke (御三家 = Owari, Kii, Mito) were granted marquess rank regardless of genmai. Asami (1994), pp. 109–110, reproduces the rule text.
The clause was written for all three sanke; Owari and Kii’s genmai were high enough to qualify for marquess on the standard rule, so only Mito triggered the actual exception. (Mito’s revenue had been substantially reduced through the 1860s; Owari and Kii were less affected.) Like Shizuoka, this is a case where the deviation is explained by a separately-written rule provision, not by Boshin alignment.

Initially ennobled count in 1884 — exactly the genmai rule’s prediction — the Fukui-Matsudaira family was promoted to marquess in 1888. The citation given was the role of the previous head 松平春嶽 (松平慶永, 1828–1890) in the Meiji Restoration. Shungaku led the public-deliberation reformist faction within the broader Tokugawa orbit, served briefly as the bakufu’s 政事総裁職 in 1862, brokered between bakufu and court during the 1864–1867 negotiations, and joined the new government’s senior advisory ranks in 1868–1872.
The 1888 timing matters: it shows the 1884 cohort was not the only ennoblement event. The Meiji peerage system allowed promotion for Restoration service on a rolling basis as historical assessments matured. Fukui is the canonical example of this rolling-promotion mechanism.

Uwajima received count rank in 1884 — exactly what the genmai rule predicts. In 1891 the family was promoted to marquess, citing the role of 伊達宗城 (1818–1892), one of the 四賢侯 (Four Wise Lords) of late-Tokugawa Japan alongside Shungaku of Fukui, 山内容堂 of Tosa, and 島津斉彬 of Satsuma. Like Shungaku, Munenari was a bridging figure during the 1860s court-bakufu negotiations and served in senior advisory roles in the early Meiji government.
The structural irony: the main Date line at Sendai received only count rank (because their post-Boshin genmai of 67,740 koku fell into the count band — itself partly a Boshin-induced territorial reduction), while the Date cadet at Uwajima was promoted past them to marquess. 18.9.4 D. Viscount → Baron (8 cases — post-1868 newly-elevated daimyo)
All eight viscount→baron downgrades share a single structural feature: each family became “true daimyo” only at the end of the Tokugawa era or at the Restoration itself. Either they were 付家老 (vassals-of-a-vassal, attached to a major-domain house) detached at the 1868 reorganisation, or they were 旗本 (direct Tokugawa banner-men) bumped above the 10,000-koku 大名 threshold in 1868–1869.
The 1884 Kazoku-rei contained a separate provision for these post-1868 elevations: rather than receiving viscount as the genmai rule would predict, they were placed at baron, reflecting their pre-Restoration baishin / hatamoto status. The downgrade is therefore explained by pre-1868 institutional history, not by Boshin alignment — five of the eight are coded anti-bakufu in our data; one is neutral; two are pro-bakufu-by-affiliation (the 付家老 cases). The pattern is orthogonal to Restoration coalition.
The eight split 4 + 4 between the two routes.

The Takenokoshi were one of three 付家老 houses attached to Owari-Tokugawa (along with Ishikawa and Naruse). Their function for centuries was to serve as senior advisors and de-facto overseers of the Owari main line on the bakufu’s behalf — a structural device by which the bakufu kept direct visibility into the major cadet branches. The Takenokoshi held about 30,000 koku in Mino (around Imao, modern Gifu) but counted as Owari baishin rather than independent daimyo. In 1868 the 付家老 system was dissolved and the three Owari attached houses became formally independent. The 1884 baron rank reflects this very recent independence.

Why baron

The most institutionally interesting case in this 8-group. The Ikoma had been daimyo of Sanuki-Takamatsu (讃岐高松藩, ~17 万石) until the 1640 御家騒動 (succession scandal), after which the main domain was confiscated and the family reduced to a small Dewa (modern Akita) fief at 8,000 koku — 旗本-equivalent status. They held this position for over two centuries.
In the Boshin War the Ikoma joined the Northern Alliance (奥羽越列藩同盟) but defected to the imperial side mid-war — one of the OREP defectors covered in chapter 02g. The new government rewarded the defection by elevating them back to 10,000-koku 大名 status in 1869. Despite this active anti-bakufu service, however, the 1884 Kazoku-rei still placed them at baron under the post-1868-elevation provision. The Ikoma case is therefore especially diagnostic: it shows the baron downgrade was structural (about pre-Restoration daimyo status) rather than discretionary (Boshin reward or punishment).

Methodological hook: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』リブロポート (1994), pp. 87–88 explicitly articulates the 現米 (genmai) threshold rule. Lebra (1993) uses the kokudaka framing. The dataset stores both deviation values side by side — the kokudaka-based (Lebra) variant and the genmai-based (Asami) variant — so the paper can present whichever baseline the analysis calls for.

title: 19  Sources & citations — the paper-writing reference
text:
19.1 The three source families
This chapter consolidates the citation chains for every paper-relevant column in analytic_wide.csv. The full column-by-column provenance lives in outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/data_provenance.md. This chapter is the narrative counterpart — what each source is, how it was acquired and verified, what variables it backs, and what to cite in the paper.
The dataset draws on three structurally different families of sources, each with its own role and citation pattern.
crumbs: 19  Sources & citations — the paper-writing reference

title: 19  Sources & citations — the paper-writing reference
text:
19.1.1 Family 1 — Hansei Ichiran 1870 (primary, project core)
The book itself: 藩制一覧 (Hansei Ichiran), 1870, two volumes, surveyed and compiled by the early Meiji government. Every hi_* column in analytic_wide.csv derives from this source, traced through the per-domain extracted chunks (extracted_chunk_vol1_t1.csv through vol2_t9.csv, plus four_domains_hansei_ichiran.csv for the hand-verified focal cases).
Citation form for the paper: Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧), 1870, vol. N, table N, page N. Compiled by the Meiji-government survey of daimyo administrative returns.
The exact page reference is in the project’s MASTER record at MASTER_hansei_ichiran.csv (one row per per-domain per-field extraction, with the source crop / page noted in the source_crop column). When a paper table cites a specific HI number, the row-level source-crop reference establishes precisely where in the original document the value lives.
Volume / table mapping (per memory reference-hansei-ichiran-kanji):

Vol. 1 (T1–T5): identifier + revenue + population tables for most domains
Vol. 2 (T6–T9): military + fiscal detail. Saga (佐嘉藩) appears only in T6; no T7/T8/T9 entry (the reference-saga-HI-gap memory documents this)



19.1.2 Family 2 — Government primary documents (paper-citable)
Three contemporaneous government compilations that overlap with HI on population and revenue, plus the official ennoblement gazettes:

19.1.2.1 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō)
Compiled by the 修史局 (Bureau of Historiography), this is the standard early-Meiji compendium of administrative data: domain populations, household counts, kokudaka, basic fiscal indicators. Our dataset uses Meiji Shiyō for the columns population_meiji_shiyo and households_meiji_shiyo.
Citation form: 明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō), Bureau of Historiography (修史局), early Meiji compendium.


19.1.2.2 大隈重信関係資料「府藩県石高人員表」
Held in the Ōkuma Shigenobu papers (大隈重信関係資料) at the Waseda University Library. The “府藩県石高人員表” is a contemporary tabulation of administrative-unit kokudaka and population. Backs the population_okuma and uchidaka_okuma_koku columns.
Citation form: 「府藩県石高人員表」, 大隈重信関係資料, Ōkuma Shigenobu papers, Waseda University Library archival collection.


19.1.2.3 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi) “人員表”
Compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫’s materials, published in Tōkei Shūshi (the early-Meiji statistical journal). Backs the population_tokei_shushi column.
Citation form: 統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi), 人員表, compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫 materials.


19.1.2.4 官報 第307号 (1884-07-08) — primary ennoblement gazette (daimyo / sekke cohort)
The government gazette that recorded the 1884-07-07 ennoblement decree — the original 公侯伯子男 (prince/marquess/count/viscount/baron) rank assignments. Available via NDL Digital Collections (pid 2943511); the project downloaded the full PDF to outputs/ndl_kanpo_307/kanpo_307_1884-07-08.pdf (23 MB, 9 pages). This is the primary record underlying every kazoku_rank_actual value (with very few exceptions handled by Kanpō #308).
Citation form: 官報 第307号 (1884-07-08), NDL pid 2943511.


19.1.2.5 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09) — primary ennoblement gazette (late cohort)
The follow-up gazette for daimyo whose rank assignments lagged the main decree by one day. Backs a small number of kazoku_rank_actual rows including 牟原藩 (= 半原藩) and several other edge cases.
Citation form: 官報 第308号 (1884-07-09), NDL Digital Collections.



19.1.3 Family 3 — Scholarly synthesis (paper-citable)
Secondary scholarly works that the project cites not as the original record but as the synthesis-and-interpretation layer between the primary documents and our values.

19.1.3.1 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』(リブロポート 1994)
The single most-important scholarly source for the paper’s central variable. Asami’s work:

Reconstructs the 1884 Kazoku-rei threshold rule with sufficient detail that the project can compute the genmai-based mechanical-expected rank (kazoku_rank_expected_genmai) for every domain.
Establishes 現米 (genmai) — not 草高 — as the threshold numerator. Pre-Asami sources (including Lebra 1993) implicitly used kokudaka as the threshold; Asami shows the rule explicitly operated on post-1869 genmai. This single point reduces the paper’s apparent “Boshin penalty” sample from 93 cases (Lebra framing) to 19 (Asami framing) by mechanically explaining 76 outcomes. The Sendai and Aizu cases that look like Restoration punishments turn out to be genmai-mechanical (see chapter 07).
Documents the explicit exception clauses (sanke clause, Tokugawa-main-line clause, Restoration-leader clause, sankyō clause, post-1868-elevation clause) — each of which explains a specific subset of deviations from the standard rule.

The book is on Jed’s shelf. Pages 87–88 articulate the genmai rule; pp. 109–110 give the sanke and sankyō clauses; pp. 105–115 the Restoration-leader exception. Asami is the citation in 94% of kazoku_rank_source rows.
Citation form: 浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』リブロポート (1994).


19.1.3.2 Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972)
The standard English-language synthesis of the Restoration period. Used for:

Domain classifications — Beasley p. xii lists “Principal Domains in 1860” with explicit 親藩 / 譜代 / 外様 designations; this is the cross-check that confirmed 仙台 (Date) and 対馬府中 (Sō) as tozama against the bakumatsu.org mis-coding.
Boshin alignment — Beasley ch. XI pp. 295–299 provides the standard chronology of Toba-Fushimi and post-Toba-Fushimi imperial consolidation. The bulk boshin_alignment_source for ~213 small viscount-tier domains cites Beasley.
Hikone defection — Beasley p. 297 is the only English-language source that explicitly names Hikone as the pre-Toba-Fushimi defector (the Ii family broke with the bakufu after Ii Naosuke’s assassination in 1860 and the subsequent Sakuradamon Incident).

Citation form: Beasley, W.G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press (1972).


19.1.3.3 Esposito, Gabriele. The Boshin War 1868–69. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530 (2020)
A military-history reference with detailed coverage of the Boshin War combatants. Used for Boshin alignment citations on the major-domain combatants (the focal-4 plus the Northern Alliance core: Aizu/斗南, Sendai, Yonezawa, Shōnai, Nagaoka, Nihonmatsu, Morioka, Kuwana). Backs 10 boshin_alignment_source rows.
Citation form: Esposito, Gabriele. The Boshin War 1868-69. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530, Osprey Publishing (2020).


19.1.3.4 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』(岩田書院 2002)
The standard Japanese-language academic monograph on the 奥羽越列藩同盟 (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei) — the Northern Alliance of bakumatsu domains that resisted the imperial advance after Toba-Fushimi. Backs the Boshin alignment citations for the 12 OREP combatants plus the 9 OREP defectors who switched sides mid-war. Surfaced 4 mis-classifications fixed in 2026-05-20 (守山, 館, 村上, 村松, 矢島 see chapter 02g).
Citation form: 工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』岩田書院 (2002).


19.1.3.5 児玉幸多 ed. 『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977)
The canonical Japanese-language han-classification compendium. The principal source for domain_category (親藩 / 譜代 / 外様 / 御三卿 / 天領). Establishes formal category designations for all ~300 domains.
Citation form: 児玉幸多 編『藩史総覧』新人物往来社 (1977).


19.1.3.6 国史大辞典 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997)
The standard Japanese-language reference dictionary. Used as the secondary verification layer for individual house histories, daimyo biographies, and domain administrative changes. Not typically cited per row (since other sources back the specific values), but listed in the paper bibliography as the reference framework.
Citation form: 国史大辞典 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997 edition).


19.1.3.7 華族大鑑刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990)
The dedicated reference for the 1884 prince-rank (公爵) ennoblements specifically — used for the 3 prince cases in our dataset (Shimazu/Satsuma, Mōri/Chōshū, Tokugawa/Shizuoka) and as a supplementary citation alongside Asami for several marquess cases.
Citation form: 華族大鑑刊行会 編『華族大鑑』(1990).


19.1.3.8 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像』中公新書 (2006)
A modern general-reader-facing book on the kazoku system as a whole. Used in 12 small viscount-tier kazoku_rank_source rows from kazoku_rank_unmatched_16.csv.
Citation form: 小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像』中公新書 (2006).


19.1.3.9 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典』吉川弘文館 (2015)
Definitive on the 1884 exclusions — daimyo families that were not ennobled because of the 改易 (kaieki, confiscation) or similar disqualifying events. Backs the documented exclusion of 堀江藩 (大沢家, 万石事件 1871) and the verification of 牟原藩 (= 半原藩, OCR-corrected). Three of the four NaN kazoku_rank_actual rows are exclusion cases documented per Matsuda 2015.
Citation form: 松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典』吉川弘文館 (2015).


19.1.3.10 Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999)
Provides the dataset’s ratio variables (kusadaka_per_commoner, retainers_per_100_commoners, gendaka_per_commoner, retainers_per_1000_koku, gendaka_to_kusadaka_ratio) from Appendix Table A1 of the book. 227 rows in our dataset have Ravina coverage. One confirmed typo: Ravina A1’s value for Akita 久保田藩 gendaka_per_commoner is 0.049 but the primary-derived value is 0.49 (decimal-place error). See memory reference-ravina-akita-typo.
Citation form: Mark Ravina. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press (1999), Appendix Table A1.


19.1.3.11 Focal-4 published-source supplements
For each of the focal-4 domains (Saga, Satsuma, Chōshū, Tosa) the project compiled a published-source supplement listing citable references for military / political / institutional details that go beyond what Hansei Ichiran provides:

outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/saga_published_sources.md — cites 藤野保『佐賀藩』(2010), Jentschura 1977 (warships), Beasley 1972
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/satsuma_published_sources.md — Esposito 2020, Jentschura 1977, Beasley 1972, Craig 1961/2000
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/choshu_published_sources.md — Craig 1961/2000, Jansen 1961/1994
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/tosa_published_sources.md — Roberts 1998, Jansen 1961/1994

Each supplement is a paper-writing-ready citation block for that focal domain’s narrative section.

The bulk-citation pattern (the 213 anti-bakufu + 20 Tokugawa-affiliate + 10 neutral) is a single Beasley-1972-chapter-XI reference that frames the standard chronology rather than enumerating each domain individually. Beasley’s analysis is that after Toba-Fushimi the imperial side issued a general directive and most small domains complied without active combatant role. Our coding follows this — listing them anti-bakufu by end-state rather than by Boshin participation.

title: 19  Sources & citations — the paper-writing reference
text:
19.5 NOT citable — internal sanity-check sources
The dataset retains two non-citable sources for internal validation only. Neither appears in any paper-citable column; both are used purely to cross-check values and to seed lookups against citable primary sources.

19.5.1 JP Wikipedia (and English Wikipedia)
Used as a finder to: - Locate primary-source candidates (e.g., a Wikipedia article cites Meiji Shiyō → check the underlying Meiji Shiyō value) - Cross-validate values that already have a citable source (e.g., Asami 1994 says marquess; JP Wiki article on the family also says marquess — confirms transcription) - Initial seed for last_lord and provincial location fields (paper-relevant) — but these need a primary citation chain to be paper-citable
Wikipedia is never citable in the paper. Per memory feedback-sourcing-constraint, the paper cites the underlying primary sources that Wikipedia references, not Wikipedia itself.


19.5.2 bakumatsu.org (内部 web scrape)
Used as an internal cross-validation layer for: - domain_category initial coding (now superseded by 児玉 1977 + Beasley 1972 re-cite as of session 2 of this project) - boshin_alignment_group initial coding (now superseded by per-row citation chain as of session 2) - last_lord and provincial fields (still partially internal-only; would need re-cite for paper inclusion)
bakumatsu.org is never citable. It is preserved in the project only because the initial scrape was the spine that allowed the bulk-data ingestion to bootstrap. All paper-relevant fields have since been re-cited.

title: 20  Data quality — what we audited, and what’s still uncertain
text:
20.1 The four audit layers
A regression on analytic_wide.csv is only as defensible as the values it ingests. This chapter walks the four-layer audit framework the project used to build confidence in the dataset, the cumulative reduction in known issues across sessions, the high-impact fixes that landed, and what remains uncertain — documented honestly rather than hidden.

The audit started at 37 flags and dropped to 26 after these fixes plus the contamination resolutions below. The remaining 26 flags are documented as definitional (e.g., 人吉藩’s high sotsu ratio is real — Hitoyoshi-Sagara had a vast sotsu-class peasant-soldier population, confirmed against Meiji Shiyō).


20.1.2 Layer 2 — Cross-page contamination audits
Hansei Ichiran is a printed book; many of its pages contain two domains stacked vertically (upper / lower halves of a page). The extraction pipeline assigns rows to one canonical domain per page based on the visible 藩 header. When a page has two domains, the assignment can drift — values from the lower domain accidentally tagged as the upper, or vice versa.

20.1.2.1 2a — Exact-match audit (stacked_page_contamination_audit.md)
The exact-match audit identifies pages containing 2+ domains, then checks whether numeric values match exactly across the suspect pairs. 261 PDF pages have 2+ domains. The audit found 22 value-collisions; 4 were confirmed contamination and fixed:








Page
Pre-fix issue
Resolution




upper_p87 和歌山 / 金澤
11 Wakayama rows duplicated Kanazawa data (Wakayama’s 草高 wrongly showed 1,353,352 — Kanazawa’s value)
Removed; Wakayama now correctly shows 草高 = 539,469 (real Kii kokudaka)


upper_p151 津山 / 津和野
6 Tsuyama rows were a mix of Tsu (人口 247k) + Tsuwano (草高 75k)
All 6 removed; Tsuyama’s true 草高 = 104,576 is on p150 (unaffected)


lower_p60 岸和田 / 菊間
7 Kishiwada rows duplicated Kikuma; Kishiwada’s real data on p58 was never extracted
7 wrong rows removed; 12 verified Kishiwada rows added from lower_p058_L crop


upper_p81 大溝 / 岡田
岡田藩’s 人口 = 11,433 was actually Ōmizo’s value; Okada’s real population (16,231) was never extracted
1 wrong row removed; 6 verified Okada rows added from upper_p081_left.jpg crop





20.1.2.2 2b — Fuzzy near-match audit (fuzzy_stacked_audit.py / .csv)
A complementary methodology with 0.5% relative tolerance — catches contamination where the values aren’t exactly equal but are very close (e.g., adjacent domains both reporting 10,000-koku-tier values). Found 8 candidates; all 8 verified as benign:

5 cases: sequential book page numbers showing up as similar values across nearby pages — incidental, not data contamination.
1 case: small-number coincidence (both values happen to be in the 100–200 range).
2 cases: Tateyama vs Tawaramoto (both 10,000-koku domains) and Himeji vs Hikone (both very large sub-territory tallies) — both confirmed as legitimate independent values that happen to be coincidentally close.



20.1.2.3 2c — The Tosa-Satsuma column-stack (separate audit)
The most-historically-instructive contamination wasn’t caught by either intra-HI audit because it involves an external primary source. Tosa’s hi_jinkou value of 896,808 is essentially identical to Satsuma’s population_tokei_shushi value of 896,817 — a column-stack OCR error in the Tōkei Shūshi tabulation pipeline. Documented in memory reference-tosa-jinkou-corruption. The project uses Meiji Shiyō’s 516,867 for Tosa instead.
A cross-source contamination audit (HI vs primary-source) would catch this pattern. It’s documented as a possible future audit; the current dataset handles the Tosa case via the memory reference.



20.1.3 Layer 3 — Ravina replication audit (ravina_replication_audit.csv)
Mark Ravina’s Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan (1999) Appendix Table A1 provides per-domain ratios (kusadaka/commoner, retainers/100 commoners, gendaka/commoner, retainers/1000 koku, gendaka/kusadaka) for ~227 domains. The project computes the same ratios both from HI directly and from primary sources (Ōkuma + Meiji Shiyō + Tōkei Shūshi), and audits the deltas against Ravina.
State after final pass (all 227 Ravina rows merged):



Match category

NO_RAVINA (not in Ravina A1)

The primary-derived ratios match Ravina substantially better than HI-derived (173 clean matches vs 78). This reflects that Ravina himself worked from the same primary sources we now cite — Meiji Shiyō and the Ōkuma papers — rather than from HI. The 12 primary-derived divergent cases were individually diagnosed in ravina_divergent_diagnoses.csv; the notable finding is that Akita’s gendaka_per_commoner = 0.049 in Ravina is a confirmed decimal-place typo (should be 0.49 per primary-derived value). See memory reference-ravina-akita-typo.


20.1.4 Layer 4 — The honest-medium tracker (honest_medium_tracker.csv)
The tracker records every value flagged at medium confidence — explicitly not claiming high-confidence accuracy, with a reason_category and reason_detail per row.
Starting at 141 medium-confidence rows in session 2 of this project, the tracker was reduced over seven cleanup rounds to 22 rows as of session 3 (2026-05-20). The remaining 22 are structural source-level limits — no further crop re-reading or OCR work will resolve them. These cells affect tail-end fiscal precision and hyper-specific demographic sub-counts. None of them feed into the paper’s IV (kokudaka) or DV (kazoku_rank_actual) or any control variable.

20.1.4.2 The 22 remaining rows by category








Reason category

SUB_KOKU_PRECISION

Sub-koku decimals (升/合/勺/才) below current crop resolution. The integer is correct; the tail digit is ambiguous between e.g. 二 vs 三. Affects 4th decimal places only.

Compound Edo-era monetary units (gold 兩/分/朱 + silver 貫/匁 + copper 文). OCR captures digits but loses which unit each digit owns.

20.1.4.3 Concrete examples (one per category)

SUB_KOKU_PRECISION: 七日市藩 内米 = 3,718.04245 koku — integer 3,718 confirmed; the .04245 vs .04235 fraction hinges on one ambiguous character. Doesn’t affect any analysis using koku-level precision.
UNIT_AMBIGUITY: 佐賀藩 雜稅金 = 10 — could be 10 ryō (gold) or 10 koku-equivalent. Raw kanji preserved but parseable only with manual interpretation.
DENSE_COLUMN_AMBIGUITY: 山崎藩 雑税金 = 27.14086 — multiple gold sub-lines on the page; column attribution not isolable.
NEEDS_HIGHER_RES: 厳原藩 寺院 = 199 — page may actually read 287 (including 盲僧/blind monks); the scope-distinguishing annotation isn’t crisply readable.
SOURCE_ILLEGIBLE: 竜野藩 穢多 = 6 — label is visible but the number is too faded to verify.

title: 20  Data quality — what we audited, and what’s still uncertain
text:
20.2 High-impact specific fixes documented
Beyond the audit-driven reductions, several specific fixes had material analytic impact and are worth flagging explicitly because they corrected values that would have entered paper tables.

20.2.1 Sendai mislabel — “兵卒 18,913” actually 卒族 demographics (Phase M)
The single highest-impact fix in the project. Sendai’s hi_total_troops_persons field had been populated with 18,913 — but the underlying source row was labeled “兵卒 (alt)” and contained 5,171 women, an impossible mix for an active military unit. Re-examination showed it was Sendai’s 卒族 (sotsu-class) household population, not troops.
Corrected to:

hi_total_troops_persons = 14,262 (correct active military personnel from the 兵士 員 row)
hi_sotsu_jinin = 18,913 (correctly bucketed as demographics)

The latent-mislabel audit was extended across all military-bucketed rows after this finding; no other mislabels of similar magnitude were found.


20.2.2 Tosa 人口 = 896,808 — column-stack OCR error from Satsuma
Tosa’s HI population value of 896,808 is an OCR column-stack error — it’s identical (within 9 persons) to Satsuma’s primary-source population, indicating the tabulation pipeline mis-aligned a column. Documented in memory reference-tosa-jinkou-corruption. The project substitutes Meiji Shiyō’s 516,867 for Tosa’s population.


20.2.3 Saga SOURCE_GAP — wholly absent from HI vol. 2 T7-T9
Saga (佐嘉藩) appears in HI vol. 2 T6 but has no T7/T8/T9 entries — the standard military and fiscal-detail tables don’t include Saga. This is a source-level gap, not an extraction failure. Documented in memory reference-saga-HI-gap; the pipeline handles via SOURCE_GAP marker rows; Saga military details come from the published-source supplement (Fujino 2010, Beasley 1972 etc. — see chapter 02 Saga walkthrough).


20.2.4 Stacked-page contamination — 4 confirmed cases (per audit Layer 2)
The 4 cross-page contamination fixes (Wakayama-Kanazawa, Tsuyama-Tsuwano, Kishiwada-Kikuma, Okada-Ōmizo) corrected 11 + 6 + 7 + 1 = 25 wrongly-attributed rows and added 12 + 6 = 18 newly-extracted verified rows. Cumulative impact on analytic_wide.csv: Wakayama’s kokudaka_iv went from a contaminated 1.35M (Kanazawa value) to its true 539,469; Okada’s hi_jinkou went from 11,433 (Ōmizo value) to its true 16,231.


20.2.5 Akita Ravina A1 typo confirmed
Ravina A1’s value for Akita 久保田藩 gendaka_per_commoner is 0.049, which is implausibly low. Primary-derived computation gives 0.457. The project documents this in memory reference-ravina-akita-typo and uses 0.49 (Ravina with decimal corrected) or the primary-derived 0.457 as the value, citing the typo explicitly.


20.2.6 4 domain-category mis-classifications
Per session 2 of this project:

仙台藩 (Date): bakumatsu.org-coded 親藩 → corrected to 外様 per Beasley p. xii + 国史大辞典
伊予吉田藩 (Date cadet): 親藩 → 外様
対馬府中藩 (Sō): 親藩 → 外様 (国主格 status but tozama)
桑名藩 (Hisamatsu-Matsudaira): 譜代 → 親藩 (Hisamatsu-Matsudaira consistency)

Audit at outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/domain_category_corrections.csv. Discussed in chapter 03c §13.8.


20.2.7 5 Boshin alignment mis-classifications
Per the same session:

守山藩, 館藩 (=松前): bakumatsu.org-coded pro-bakufu → corrected to anti-bakufu (OREP defectors per 工藤威 2002)
村上藩, 村松藩: anti-bakufu → pro-bakufu (OREP combatants per 工藤威 2002)
淀藩: neutral → anti-bakufu (Toba-Fushimi castle-gate refusal 1868-01-28, per JP Wiki + Boshin War Wikipedia citation chain)

section: 20.4 How to characterise this work for paper reviewers
text:
20.4 How to characterise this work for paper reviewers
Three framings the paper can use:

“Replication-audited primary-source dataset”: every paper-relevant variable has a citable primary source (or scholarly-synthesis source); the values were cross-validated against Ravina (1999) at the ratio level; cross-page contamination was audited and surfaced 4 cases that were corrected.
“Transparent honest-medium tracking”: the project maintains a public per-cell tracker (honest_medium_tracker.csv) documenting every value that is not high-confidence, with the reason. Most empirical Meiji-history work either doesn’t disclose this or absorbs it silently; we surface it.
“Round-by-round documented cleanup history”: the seven cleanup rounds (141 → 22 medium-confidence rows) are each documented in git commit history and in honest_medium_tracker_summary.md, with reproducible scripts in outputs/scripts/ for every batch fix. A reviewer can trace any specific value’s provenance back to the source crop and to the cleanup decision that affected it.


Audit infrastructure:

outputs/scripts/sanity_check_audit.py — Layer 1 (within-domain ratio sanity)
outputs/scripts/stacked_page_contamination_audit.md + fuzzy_stacked_audit.py/.csv — Layer 2 (cross-page contamination)
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/ravina_replication_audit.csv + ravina_divergent_diagnoses.csv — Layer 3 (Ravina cross-validation)
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/honest_medium_tracker.csv + honest_medium_tracker_summary.md — Layer 4 (medium-confidence tracker)

Cumulative documentation:

outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/data_provenance.md — per-column citation guidance
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/sanity_check_audit.csv — current sanity-check flag state (26 active)
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/stacked_page_contamination_audit.md — exact-match contamination findings
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/complete_military_spot_check.md — 8 COMPLETE military rows verified
outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/hi_omitted_partial_analysis.md — 14 HI-omitted-partial domains analyzed

The full git commit log under outputs/hansei_ichiran_reocr/ is the round-by-round audit trail.

title: Bibliography
text:
Primary sources
This page aggregates every published source the dataset draws on. For per-row citations, see the matching source columns in the project’s analytic table.
Hansei Ichiran (藩制一覧). 1870. Two-volume government survey of all daimyo domains, compiled in the immediate pre-haihan-chiken period. National Diet Library Digital Collections. — The project’s primary han-level source for every HI-derived field.
官報 第307号 (Kanpō #307). 1884-07-08. The Imperial government gazette publishing the 1884-07-07 kazoku ennoblement decree + initial 509-house roster. NDL Digital Collections pid 2943511. Downloaded locally to outputs/ndl_kanpo_307/. — Primary citation for the 1884 kazoku rank.
官報 第308号 (Kanpō #308). 1884-07-09. Continuation of the same gazette with the late-cohort ennoblements (kuge, etc.). NDL pid 2943512.
明治史要 (Meiji Shiyō). 1870s. Compendium compiled by the Bureau of Historiography (修史局). — Primary source for the Meiji Shiyō population, household-count, and status-class breakdown fields.
大隈重信関係資料「府藩県石高人員表」 (Ōkuma Shigenobu papers — Fu-Han-Ken Kokudaka Jin’in-hyō). Waseda University Library archival. — Primary source for the Ōkuma uchidaka and population fields.
統計集誌 (Tōkei Shūshi). Compiled by 呉文聡 from 野村文夫 materials. — Primary source for the Tōkei Shūshi population field.

title: Bibliography
text:
Secondary scholarly sources — Japanese
浅見雅男『華族誕生 名誉と体面の明治』. リブロポート, 1994. — Backs 94% of the 1884 kazoku-rank citations. Articulates the 現米 (genmai) threshold rule that reduces apparent rank-deviations from 93 (kokudaka-based) to 19 (genmai-based).
華族大鑑刊行会編『華族大鑑』. 1990. — Primary scholarly source for prince-rank ennoblements (20 rows).
工藤威『奥羽列藩同盟の基礎的研究』. 岩田書院, 2002. — Authoritative scholarly roster of the Northern Alliance (Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei), 31 members. Citable via the JP Wikipedia 奥羽越列藩同盟 article which transcribes the Kudō roster.
児玉幸多 ed.『藩史総覧』. 新人物往来社, 1977. — Canonical han-classification reference for the Tokugawa-era category field (親藩 / 譜代 / 外様 / 御三卿 / 天領).
国史大辞典. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1979–1997. — Definitive secondary on Japanese historical terminology + domain classifications.
小田部雄次『華族 近代日本貴族の虚像と実像』. 中公新書, 2006. — Secondary kazoku reference, especially for small-domain edge cases.
森岡浩『日本名門・名家大辞典』. 東京堂出版, 2012. — Small-domain rank references.
松田敬之『〈華族爵位〉請願人名辞典』. 吉川弘文館, 2015. — Definitive 1884 exclusions catalog (改易 cases like 大沢家/堀江藩, 林家/請西藩).
藤野保『佐賀藩』. 2010. — Saga-specific reference (used because 佐嘉藩 is absent from HI vol. 2 T7–T9).

title: Bibliography
text:
Secondary scholarly sources — English
Beasley, W. G. The Meiji Restoration. Stanford University Press, 1972. — Standard scholarly history. Ch. XI for Boshin alignment + Principal Domains in 1860 (p. xii) for Tokugawa-era category assignments.
Esposito, Gabriele & Rava, Giuseppe. Japanese Armies 1868–1877: The Boshin War and Satsuma Rebellion. Osprey Men-at-Arms 530. Bloomsbury, 2020. — Military narrative + key Boshin combatants. Citable for the major focal-4 + Northern Alliance core.
Lebra, Takie Sugiyama. Above the Clouds: Status Culture of the Modern Japanese Nobility. University of Hawai’i Press, 1993. — Threshold-rule framing (kokudaka-based, which Asami 1994 corrects to genmai-based).
Ravina, Mark. Land and Lordship in Early Modern Japan. Stanford University Press, 1999. — Appendix Table A1 ratios. Cited for the per-capita ratio fields. Known typo: Akita 久保田藩 gendaka-per-commoner 0.049 should be 0.49 (decimal-place error).
Craig, A. M. Choshu in the Meiji Restoration. Harvard University Press, 1961 / 2000. — Choshu-specific military supplement.
Roberts, Luke S. Mercantilism in a Japanese Domain: The Merchant Origins of Economic Nationalism in 18th-Century Tosa. Cambridge University Press, 1998. — Tosa supplement.
Jansen, Marius B. The Making of Modern Japan. Harvard / Belknap, 2000. — General history; also Jansen (1961/1994) Sakamoto Ryōma and the Meiji Restoration.
Drea, Edward J. Japan’s Imperial Army: Its Rise and Fall, 1853–1945. University Press of Kansas, 2009.
Jentschura, H., Jung, D. & Mickel, P. Warships of the Imperial Japanese Navy, 1869–1945. Naval Institute Press, 1977. — For HI warship data verification.

title: Bibliography
text:
Data sources (not citable but documented)
Harvard CHGIS 1664 daimyo shapefile. — Used for the map polygons (approximation, since 1664 boundaries were generally stable through 1870).
bakumatsu.org web scrape (han_dataset_work/data/bakumatsu_online.csv). — Originally used as a finder for Tokugawa-era category, Boshin alignment, and last-lord identity. NOT directly citable for the paper; values cross-validated against Beasley 1972, 工藤 2002, 児玉 ed. 1977 etc.
Wikipedia 府藩県三治制 scrape (han_dataset_work/data/wiki_fuhanken.csv). — Web-scraped Japanese Wikipedia 府藩県三治制 article. NOT directly citable; the article’s underlying primary sources (Meiji Shiyō / Ōkuma / Tōkei Shūshi) are what the project actually cites for each field.
